content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

This letter supersedes the Miller Advice Letter, No. I-09-253, in part.

September 22, 2010

Daniel Miller
Filing Officer
Marin County Registrar of Voters
P.O. Box E
San Rafael, CA 94913

Re:    The Miller Advice Letter, No. I-09-253, dated December 14, 2009.
       Our File No. I-09-253(a)

Dear Mr. Miller:

       This letter serves to clarify assistance provided in the Miller Advice Letter, No. I-09-253
(copy enclosed) regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act"),¹ and supersedes our prior letter to the extent that the letter finds that a city may transfer
its city clerk's duties as a filing officer to a county election's official without restriction.

QUESTION

       May local jurisdictions establish a single central filing agency to review and process
campaign statements for both city and county jurisdictions?²

CONCLUSION

       With respects to candidates and committees within the jurisdiction, a local jurisdiction
may transfer its filing officer's duties to a central filing agency. However, a local jurisdiction

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² In your previous request for assistance, you also asked whether local jurisdictions may pass regulations
or ordinances that specify filing requirements and establish local enforcement procedures for the failure to file,
or ordinances that specify filing requirements and establish local enforcement proceedings for the failure to file,
which supersede enforcement proceedings set forth in the Act. However, we do not address this question herein,
and our previous assistance regarding this question is unaffected by our current analysis.

File No. I-09-253(a)
Page No. 2

that designates an agency outside of the jurisdiction as its filing officer is ultimately responsible
for ensuring that the agency fulfills its duties as the jurisdiction's filing officer, and both the local
jurisdiction and the designated agency are jointly and severally liable for any violations of the
Act resulting from their arrangement. Moreover, a local jurisdiction that designates an agency
outside of the jurisdiction as its filing officer must also ensure public access to filings by
designating an agency within the jurisdiction to collect and maintain copies of filings from the
designated filing officer for public inspection.

FACTS

You are the filing officer for the County of Marin and previously sought assistance as to
whether the Act permits local jurisdictions to establish a single central filing agency to review
and process campaign finance statements for both city and county jurisdictions. In response to
your inquiry, we issued an analysis of your question in the Miller Advice Letter, supra, on
December 14, 2009. Notwithstanding our prior analysis, this letter serves to clarify our previous
assistance and supersedes our prior letter to the extent that the letter finds that a city may transfer
its city clerk's duties as a filing officer to a county election's official without restriction.

ANALYSIS

As addressed in our previous letter, the controlling provision of the Act provides the
following:

"Nothing in this title prevents the Legislature or any other state or local
agency from imposing additional requirements on any person if the requirements
do not prevent the person from complying with this title. If any act of the
Legislature conflicts with the provisions of this title, this title shall prevail."
(Section 81013.)

Additionally, Section 81009.5(b) provides:

"Notwithstanding Section 81013, no local government agency shall enact
any ordinance imposing filing requirements additional to or different from those
set forth in Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 84100) for elections held in its
jurisdiction unless the additional or different filing requirements apply only to the
candidates seeking election in that jurisdiction, their controlled committees or
committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose their candidacies,
and to committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose a candidate
or to support or oppose the qualification of, or passage of, a local ballot measure
which is being voted on only in that jurisdiction, and to city or county general
purpose committees active only in that city or county, respectively."

As a general rule, a local jurisdiction is permitted to impose additional requirements so
long as the requirements do not prevent a person from complying with the Act and apply only to

File No. I-09-253(a)
Page No. 3

candidates and committees within the jurisdiction. (Sections 81013 and 81009.5.)³ However,
when provisions of local law conflict with the state law, the appropriate provisions of state law
supersede them. (See Cederdahl Advice Letter, No. I-92-467, citing Cohen v. Board of
Supervisors (1985) 40 Cal.3d 277 and In re Hubbard (1964) 62 Cal.2d 119.) In many cases,
therefore, a local government agency may not enact an ordinance that differs from and
supersedes the Act. (In re Miller (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 9l.) To make this determination, the
provisions of the local law must be examined on a provision-by-provision basis.

Under some circumstances, a local requirement might be invalid under Section 81013
even if an ordinance is consistent with the Act. For example, we have advised that when local
filing requirements become so "burdensome or confusing" that they seriously interfere with
filings required by the Act, the state law preempts the local ordinance. (Nielsen Advice Letter,
No. A-82-006.)

Turning more specifically to the filing location for campaign statements, Section 84215
provides the following in pertinent part:

"All candidates and elected officers and their controlled committees,
except as provided in subdivision (e), shall file one copy of the campaign
statements required by Section 84200 with the elections official of the county in
which the candidate or elected official is domiciled, as defined in subdivision (b)
of Section 349 of the Elections Code. In addition, campaign statements shall be
filed at the following places:

"(a) Statewide elected officers and candidates for these offices other than
the Board of Equalization, supreme court justices, their controlled committees,
committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose these candidates,
elected officers, supreme court justices, or statewide measures, or the
qualification of state ballot measures, and all state general purpose committees
and filers not specified in subdivisions (b) to (e), inclusive:

"(1) The original and one copy with the Secretary of State.

"(2) One copy with the Registrar-Recorder of Los Angeles County.

"(3) One copy with the Registrar of Voters of the City and County of San
Francisco.

³ Notably, charter cities are granted "home rule" powers under Article XI, Section 5 of the state
Constitution. Under this authority, a charter city's regulation or ordinance implicating a "municipal affair" is
superseded by state law only if there is an actual conflict and only if the state statute is one of "statewide concern"
that is "reasonably related" and "narrowly tailored" to its resolution. (See Johnson v. Bradley (1992) 4 Cal. 4ᵗʰ 389,
399.) At this time, we express no opinion as to whether charter cities may impose additional filing requirements,
other than those specified in the Act, upon persons or committees active within the jurisdiction but not a committee
subject to filing with the city under Section 84215(e).

File No. I-09-253(a)
Page No. 4

"(b) Members of the Legislature or Board of Equalization, court of appeal
justices, superior court judges, candidates for those offices, their controlled
committees, and committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose
these candidates or officeholders:

"(1) The original and one copy with the Secretary of State.

"(2) One copy with the elections official of the county with the largest number of
registered voters in the districts affected.

"(c) Elected officers in jurisdictions other than legislative districts, Board
of Equalization districts, or appellate court districts that contain parts of two or
more counties, candidates for these offices, their controlled committees, and
committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose candidates or local
measures to be voted upon in one of these jurisdictions shall file the original and
one copy with the elections official of the county with the largest number of
registered voters in the jurisdiction.

"(d) County elected officers, candidates for these offices, their controlled
committees, committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose
candidates or local measures to be voted upon in any number of jurisdictions
within one county, other than those specified in subdivision (e), and county
general purpose committees shall file the original and one copy with the elections
official of the county.

"(e) City elected officers, candidates for city office, their controlled
committees, committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose
candidates or local measures to be voted upon in one city, and city general
purpose committees shall file the original and one copy with the clerk of the city.
These elected officers, candidates, and committees need not file with the elections
official of the county in which they are domiciled."

In reexamining our previous advice, we have an opportunity to take a closer look at
whether a local jurisdiction transferring the duties of its filing clerk to an agency outside of the
jurisdiction conflicts with the provisions of the Act. Notwithstanding the filing locations
specified in Section 84215, the Act does permit local jurisdictions to designate a different filing
officer in certain circumstances. For instance, Section 82009.5 defines the term clerk as the "city
or county clerk unless the city council or board of supervisors has designated any other agency to
perform the specified function." Additionally, for purposes of candidates and committees solely
within the City and County of San Francisco, we have permitted the City and County of San
Francisco to designate the San Francisco Ethics Commission as the filing location for campaign
reports and statements in lieu of the city clerk as "a valid exercise of a local jurisdiction's
authority under Section 81009.5." (Sutton Advice Letter, No. A-01-184.)

File No. I-09-253(a)
Page No. 5

However, a local jurisdiction's authority to designate a different filing officer is not
without restriction. We have previously determined that the Act prohibited a charter amendment
by the City and County of San Francisco to designate the San Francisco Ethics Commission as
the filing location for campaign reports and statements in lieu of the city clerk to the extent that
the amendment applied "not only to local persons, but also [to] persons operating campaigns on a
statewide level." (Moll Advice Letter, A-96-315.)

Examining specifically the question of whether a city can designate a filing location other
than a city agency, we found in our previous response, Miller Advice Letter, supra, that cities
may transfer their clerks' duties as filing officers to other counties in which the cities are located
or joint agencies of the cities and the counties in which the cities are located. This conclusion
rested largely on the fact that city clerks' duties as filing officers are limited to candidates and
committees within their respective cities under Section 84215(e). In addition, we are sensitive to
the fact that permitting consolidation to a centralized filing location provides local jurisdictions
with some leeway in allocating their resources in the most efficient manner. However, the
practice of a local jurisdiction transferring its clerk's duties to an agency outside of the
jurisdiction, even if limited to those candidates and committees operating solely within that
jurisdiction, raises significant concerns regarding both the lack of accountability and the
hindrance of public access.

Ultimately, local jurisdictions bear the responsibility of ensuring compliance with the Act
for those candidates and committees that file campaign statements and reports with the
jurisdiction pursuant to Section 84215. A local jurisdiction is not absolved of these
responsibilities merely because it has designated an agency outside of the jurisdiction to serve as
its filing officer. Thus, a local jurisdiction that has designated an agency outside of the
jurisdiction to serve as its filing officer must take all appropriate precautions to ensure that the
agency is fulfilling its duties as the jurisdiction's designated filing officer. Moreover pursuant
to Section 91006, both the local jurisdiction and any agency serving as the jurisdiction's filing
officer under an agreement with the jurisdiction are jointly and severally liable for any violation
of the Act resulting from the arrangement.

In addition, a local jurisdiction that has designated an agency outside of the jurisdiction to
serve as its filing officer must also ensure that public access to campaign statements and reports
is not hindered by its designation. As expressed in Section 81002(a), one of the primary
purposes of the Act is to ensure that, "Receipts and expenditures in election campaigns should
be fully and truthfully disclosed in order that the voters may be fully informed and improper
practices may be inhibited." To serve this purpose, it is essential that every jurisdiction maintain
a copy of all campaign statements and reports required to be filed with the jurisdiction under
Section 84215(e) and allow public access to those reports through the jurisdiction. While we do
not find that the Act mandates a strict prohibition against a local jurisdiction designating an
agency outside of the jurisdiction to act as its filing officer, this arrangement is not consistent
with the purposes of the Act, and therefore prohibited, unless the jurisdiction ensures public
access to the filings by also designating an agency within the jurisdiction to collect and maintain
copies of all filings from the designated filing officer for public inspection.

File No. I-09-253(a)
Page No. 6


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
